title: Neonatal resuscitation in the delivery room (NRP/AAP)
executiveSummary: Resuscitate newborns weighing 500 g to 2500 g (days 0–30) in the delivery room following the NRP/AAP algorithm, consisting of an initial assessment, positive-pressure ventilation, chest compressions if HR < 60 bpm, and umbilical vascular access without delaying effective ventilation.
body:
## Steps to perform

1. Before delivery: confirm estimated gestational age, perinatal risk, and complete NRP equipment availability (radiant warmer, HR monitor, pulse oximeter, T-piece resuscitator or self-inflating bag with a PEEP valve, laryngoscope blades 0/00, endotracheal tubes 2.5–3.0 mm, stylets, UVC/UAC insertion kits, thermometer).
2. At birth: dry vigorously, remove the wet blanket, place under the radiant warmer; position the neck in slight extension (sniffing position); suction the mouth and nares only if secretions are obstructing or in the presence of a non-vigorous infant with meconium (avoid routine deep suctioning).
3. Clamp and cut the umbilical cord at ≥ 30–60 s if the newborn is breathing or crying and the cord stump is pink; in infants < 34 weeks or those requiring immediate resuscitation, assess delayed cord clamping according to institutional protocol.
4. Evaluate breathing and HR at 30 seconds: if breathing/crying and HR ≥ 100 bpm → initiate skin-to-skin contact with the mother, thermoregulation, and close observation; if not → initiate positive-pressure ventilation (PPV) within ≤ 30 s.
5. Place a pulse oximeter probe on the preductal right hand; adjust FiO₂ according to NRP targets per minute of life (initiate at 21–30% in ≥ 35 weeks; 21–30% in 28–34 weeks; consider titrated oxygen in < 28 weeks).
6. PPV: ensure a tight mask seal, rate of 40–60/min, initial pressure of 20–25 cmH₂O (adjust to achieve visible chest rise); use PEEP at 5 cmH₂O if the equipment permits.
7. Reevaluate every 30 seconds: if HR ≥ 100 bpm and spontaneous breathing occurs → discontinue PPV, continue thermal support, and consider titrated oxygen; if HR < 100 bpm or if apneic → continue PPV; if no clinical improvement is observed → consider a Guedel airway, equipment adjustment (T-piece preferred), or endotracheal intubation (ETT) using a 2.5–3.0 mm tube.
8. If HR remains < 60 bpm after ≥ 30 s of effective PPV, initiate chest compressions at a 3:1 ratio (90 compressions + 30 ventilations/min with two rescuers) or 15:2 if intubated and performed by two trained rescuers.
9. After 60 seconds of compressions: if HR ≥ 60 bpm, halt compressions and optimize ventilation; if HR < 60 bpm → administer epinephrine 0.01–0.03 mg/kg IV/IO/UVC (0.1–0.3 ml/kg of a 1:10,000 solution) every 3–5 min; consider a fluid bolus of 10 ml/kg if hypovolemia is suspected.
10. Cannulate an emergency UVC if vascular access is absent; do not interrupt PPV for > 10 s to perform procedures; document times, FiO₂, SpO₂, medications, and response.
11. Upon stabilization: maintain an axillary temperature of 36.5–37.5 °C, ensure continuous monitoring, obtain a blood gas analysis, and document transfer to the Neonatal ICU.
12. Conduct an immediate team debriefing and document the event in the neonatal medical history.

## Scientific rationale

### Population and scope

Strictly neonatal protocol: newborns from day 0 to day 30 of life, weighing 500 g to 2500 g, in the delivery room or perinatal resuscitation area. Do not extrapolate pediatric or adult algorithms.

### Indications

- Respiratory depression or HR < 100 bpm at birth.
- Newborns < 34 weeks requiring thermal support and close evaluation.
- Meconium-stained fluid with a non-vigorous newborn.
- History of fetal distress, emergency cesarean section, or known congenital malformations.

### Evidence and recommendations

> **Source:** NRP/AAP (8th ed.) — The most frequent cause of neonatal bradycardia is hypoxia; effective ventilation is the single most important intervention. Initiate chest compressions only if HR < 60 bpm despite ≥ 30 s of adequate PPV (American Academy of Pediatrics, 2021).

> **Source:** VON — Reducing hypothermia and delays in initiating PPV improves clinical outcomes in preterm infants < 1500 g (Vermont Oxford Network, 2024).

### Preductal SpO₂ targets (NRP)

| Minute of life | Approximate target SpO₂ |
| --- | --- |
| 1 | 60–65% |
| 2 | 65–70% |
| 3 | 70–75% |
| 4 | 75–80% |
| 5 | 80–85% |
| 10 | 85–95% |

### Initial ventilation parameters

| Weight / GA | ETT size (mm) | ETT depth (cm) | Initial PPV pressure |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| < 1000 g / < 28 wk | 2.5 | 6–7 | 20–25 cmH₂O |
| 1000–2000 g | 2.5–3.0 | 7–8 | 20–25 cmH₂O |
| 2000–2500 g | 3.0 | 8–9 | 25–30 cmH₂O |

### Resuscitation medications

| Medication | Dose | Route | Notes |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Epinephrine | 0.01–0.03 mg/kg | IV/UVC/IO | Repeat every 3–5 min |
| Volume (NS) | 10 ml/kg | Slow IV | If hypovolemia is probable |
| 10% Glucose | 2 ml/kg | Slow IV | If blood glucose is < 25 mg/dl (confirmed) |

### Criteria for discontinuing resuscitation

Determined according to institutional protocol, gestational age, birth weight, and family expectations; requires a documented senior medical decision.

### Complications — notify immediately

- Inability to ventilate (airway obstruction, congenital airway malformation).
- Persistent HR < 60 bpm despite adequate PPV, chest compressions, and epinephrine.
- Hypothermia (< 36 °C) or hyperthermia (> 37.5 °C) post-resuscitation.
- Pneumothorax, pulmonary hemorrhage, or subcutaneous emphysema.
- Need for ECMO or urgent transfer to a tertiary care center.

bibliography.citation: American Academy of Pediatrics. Neonatal Resuscitation Program (NRP) — 8th Edition Provider Manual and Algorithm.
Vermont Oxford Network. Evidence-based quality improvement for newborn care in NICUs.
World Health Organization. Guidelines on basic newborn resuscitation and immediate newborn care.
Wyckoff MH, et al. Part 13: Neonatal Resuscitation — 2020 American Heart Association Guidelines. Circulation. 2020.
Sociedad Argentina de Terapia Intensiva (SATI). Consensos en neonatología y cuidados críticos perinatales.
